Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd mostly just listen to lecture recordings when I felt like it, usually while doing other things like commuting or cleaning my room. I know I should have been more organized, but I just couldn't bring myself to sit down and plan things out. When I did study, it was usually in random bursts when something caught my interest or when I remembered an assignment was coming up.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly spend a whole evening catching up on recordings. Deadlines definitely pushed me to do more, but even then I'd leave things until pretty late. I guess I just went with whatever felt manageable in the moment rather than sticking to any schedule.
Q3: Usually it was because I got a notification about something due soon, or I'd suddenly remember I hadn't checked in for a while and feel a bit guilty. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there was anything new, scroll around for a few minutes, then close it. It wasn't really a habit, more like a sporadic thing when I couldn't avoid it anymore.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were maybe a couple of weeks early on where I tried to check in regularly, but that didn't last. The LMS just felt like another thing on my to-do list that I kept pushing aside. I never got into a flow where it felt natural to log in every day or anything like that.
Q5: The lecture recordings were the most useful for me since I learn better by listening anyway. I could play them back, pause when I needed to think, and listen again if something didn't click the first time. The discussion posts and reading materials didn't do much for me—I found them kind of dry and hard to get through.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the discussion forums and a lot of the supplementary readings. They just felt like busywork to me, and I couldn't motivate myself to engage with them when I was already bored with the course. The videos I'd sometimes skim through but not really watch properly unless they seemed directly relevant to an assignment.
Q7: If I could see how it connected to something I'd be assessed on, that made it feel worthwhile. Or if the content itself was actually interesting and presented in a way that held my attention. A lot of stuff just felt like filler though, and I couldn't justify spending my limited energy on things that seemed pointless.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me. If it wasn't required, I just wouldn't do it—I had enough trouble keeping up with the mandatory things. Required activities at least had some external pressure attached, which was sometimes the only thing that got me moving. Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone when I was out and about. I also used some YouTube videos and podcasts that explained concepts better than the course materials sometimes. My notes were mostly voice memos I'd record to myself summarizing what I'd learned.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened while I was listening to downloaded recordings during my commute or while doing chores. I'd also sometimes study with a friend from another course who'd explain things to me verbally. None of that would show up anywhere in the system, but it's where a lot of my understanding actually came from.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture once and then listen to it multiple times over the next few days without ever logging back in. The LMS would just show one access, but I might have gone through that material three or four times offline. It was easier than streaming everything repeatedly.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I just couldn't be bothered. I think the course content itself didn't grab me, and when you're already feeling bored, everything feels like a chore. My anxiety would spike a bit when assignments piled up, but mostly I just felt disconnected from the whole thing.
Q13: Yeah, I work part-time and money's tight, so sometimes I was just too tired after shifts to engage with coursework properly. When I was exhausted or had other things going on, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Confusion about some topics also made me avoid them rather than ask for help, which probably wasn't smart.
Q14: Plenty of times. I'd open a video or reading and just zone out completely, not really absorbing anything. My mind would wander and I'd realize I'd been staring at the screen for twenty minutes without taking anything in. It felt like going through the motions without actually being present.
Q15: I put off discussion posts constantly because they felt awkward and pointless to me. I knew they were supposed to help with engagement or whatever, but I just couldn't make myself care enough. Some readings I'd avoid too because they seemed dense and I told myself I'd get to them later, which usually meant never.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me actually submit work. I'd suddenly kick into gear a day or two before something was due and cram to get it done. Without that pressure, I probably wouldn't have completed half of what I did. It's not a great system, but it worked well enough for my grades.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it made me feel like maybe I wasn't as lost as I thought, which was reassuring. But it didn't really change how I engaged day-to-day. I'd read the comments, feel okay about myself for a bit, then go back to my usual avoidance patterns. The feedback was nice but not motivating enough to change my habits.
Q18: The structure was pretty flexible, which sounds good but actually made it easier for me to drift. If there had been more regular check-ins or smaller deadlines throughout, I might have stayed more on track. The way it was set up, I could coast for weeks and then panic-catch-up, which is exactly what I did.
Q19: Having audio options for lectures made things easier for me since that's how I learn best. But the heavy reliance on written discussion posts and reading materials made engagement harder because those formats just don't work for me. I wish there had been more variety in how we could demonstrate understanding.
Q20: I think some people might be searching for things, trying to figure out what's expected, or just clicking around without really knowing what to do. Or they could be anxious and checking everything repeatedly without actually making progress on submissions. High activity doesn't mean productive activity.
Q21: That's pretty much me, honestly. I barely showed up in the LMS but I still did well on exams and assignments because I was learning in other ways. Listening to recordings offline, talking things through with friends, using external resources—none of that shows up in the system but it's how I actually learned the material.
Q22: Course logs can show when you clicked on things and how long you might have had something open, but they can't tell if you were actually paying attention or understanding anything. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with peers, the thinking you do while walking around. They're pretty surface-level, really.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just going through the motions to pass. It's about being mentally present and curious, not just physically logging in. I don't think I was truly engaged with this course—I was just doing enough to get by.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I actually did care about doing well, even if I looked disengaged on paper. It wouldn't capture my anxiety about not being more involved, or the fact that I learned most effectively through methods that don't leave digital traces. My grades reflect understanding the system missed entirely.
